update-prices.label: Update prices
update-prices.summary: Practice updating prices in the inventory_items table using safe SQL habits. Learn to preview affected rows, use WHERE clauses, and avoid accidental data changes.

q1.title: Update price for a specific item

q3.title: Update prices for test items only

q1.title: Update price for a specific item
q1.prompt: Increase the price of the item named 'Sticker Pack' by $2 in the inventory_items table.

q4.title: Safe update practices
q4.prompt: Which of the following are good practices when updating prices in a table? Select all that apply.
q4.hint: Think about how to avoid accidental changes.

help.concept: Previewing and filtering updates helps prevent mistakes. Avoid updating all rows unless intentional.
help.hint_1: Preview with SELECT and use WHERE to target updates.
help.hint_2: Avoid running UPDATE without WHERE unless you want to change every row.

options.a: a. Preview affected rows with SELECT before running UPDATE
options.b: b. Always use a WHERE clause to target specific rows
options.c: c. Run UPDATE without WHERE to save time
options.d: d. Double-check your filter conditions before executing UPDATE

q5.title: Fill in the blank: WHERE clause importance
q5.prompt: Choose the best value for the first missing blank in the statement.
q5.hint: This clause limits which rows are affected.

help.concept: The the missing term clause specifies which rows to update, preventing accidental changes to all rows.
help.hint_1: It's the clause that filters rows in UPDATE and SELECT.
help.hint_2: Without this clause, every row in the table will be updated.

sk1.title: Preview Before Update
sk1.bodyMarkdown:
Before updating prices in the `inventory_items` table, always preview the rows you plan to change. For example, to see all items in the 'Accessories' category:

```sql
SELECT id, name, price FROM inventory_items WHERE category = 'Accessories';
```

This helps you confirm which rows will be affected before running an UPDATE.

sk2.title: Updating Prices Safely
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
To update prices, use the `UPDATE` statement with a `WHERE` clause. For example, to increase the price of all 'Kitchen' items by $1:

```sql
UPDATE inventory_items SET price = price + 1 WHERE category = 'Kitchen';
```

Always include a `WHERE` clause to avoid changing every row by mistake.
